body_paragraphs: Oil crossed $100 a barrel for the first time since May - Brent closed Thursday at $100.69, up about 7%, after Iran struck a Kuwaiti airbase and the Houthis hit two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea. Stocks took it directly: the S&P fell 1.21%, the Nasdaq 2.15% on top of a 14.5% Tesla drop and a 7% Alphabet slide after earnings, and the 10-year yield jumped to 4.70% - its highest since January 2025. FOMC hike odds for next week's meeting roughly doubled overnight, to about 35%. None of that was priced in a week ago; five days out, the Fed's job just got harder.

Locally, the Heat's accidental leak keeps looking less accidental - the press-conference page was real prep work, just published early - and Kalshi bettors now put Miami's odds of landing LeBron at 51%, up from 39%, though his agent says there's still no timetable. The Marlins get their first game back tonight after the nine-game skid, hosting the Padres at loanDepot Park with the wild-card cushion long gone.

Beyond the feed: an Aeon essay out this week argues the 'ancient world' most of us were taught is a fraction of the real one, if you're willing to look past Greece and Rome. And Miami Beach's Art Deco district is quietly marking its own centennial - a hundred years since the 1926 hurricane forced the rebuild that became the neighborhood's whole identity. Worth a slower read this weekend.

what_changed.title: Oil breaks $100 for the first time since May
what_changed.previous_state: As of Wednesday, Brent had closed at $94.13, a five-week high, with the ceasefire proposal stalled and Strait of Hormuz transits down to 25 ships.
what_changed.current_state: Brent settled at $100.69 Thursday, up about 7%, after Iran struck Kuwait's Al-Salem airbase and the Houthis hit two Saudi Arabian tankers in the Red Sea - the 13th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran.
what_changed.why_it_matters: This crossed from a contained strike campaign into a regional supply threat touching Kuwait and Saudi shipping directly - the scenario that breaks inflation forecasts.

what_changed.title: Hike odds more than double overnight
what_changed.previous_state: CME FedWatch had hike odds at roughly 16.6% as of Wednesday, dispersed but stable across sources.
what_changed.current_state: Odds moved to roughly 35% by Thursday as oil-driven inflation risk collided with a heavy earnings week - more than double in a single session.
what_changed.why_it_matters: Five days from the meeting, the Fed now has to weigh a genuine oil shock against an economy that was cooling on its own terms.

what_changed.title: Stocks drop hardest in weeks on oil plus earnings
what_changed.previous_state: Wednesday's session had already reversed Tuesday's chip-earnings rally (S&P -0.14%, Nasdaq -0.56%).
what_changed.current_state: Thursday's drop was sharper: S&P -1.21% to 7,408.30, Nasdaq -2.15% to 25,137.69, with Tesla down 14.5% on an earnings miss and Alphabet down 7% after raising 2026 capex guidance to $195-205 billion.
what_changed.why_it_matters: Oil, rates, and now AI-capex anxiety are all pulling the same direction at once - a harder mix to underwrite against than any one of them alone.

what_changed.title: Kalshi moves the Heat to 51% on LeBron
what_changed.previous_state: As of Wednesday, the leaked presser link was reported as a staffing mistake, with ESPN naming Miami one of three teams LeBron was considering.
what_changed.current_state: The Heat confirmed the presser page itself was real prep work - built in case of a signing - just published early; Kalshi bettors pushed Miami's odds to 51%, up from 39% before the leak, though agent Rich Paul says there's no timetable.
what_changed.why_it_matters: Betting markets are now pricing this as more likely than not, against a roster that's already hard-capped - the math someone has to make work just got more real.

what_changed.title: Marlins open the Padres series tonight
what_changed.previous_state: Miami was off Thursday after the nine-game skid, set to host the Padres starting Friday.
what_changed.current_state: Miami (52-51, 8.5 games back) hosts San Diego tonight at loanDepot Park, first pitch 7:10 PM, with German Marquez starting for the Padres - the first game since the skid-ending sweep loss.
what_changed.why_it_matters: The wild-card cushion is gone; tonight is the first real read on whether the off day changed anything.

what_changed.title: CRE deal volume down 24% year over year
what_changed.previous_state: No prior tracking of 2026 CRE capital-markets volume in this edition's threads.
what_changed.current_state: CBRE's Q2 2026 capital markets update shows deal volume down 24% year over year, construction spending 2.7% below plan, and hiring slowing across the sector - guidance now favors disciplined underwriting over volume for the second half.
what_changed.why_it_matters: This is the environment behind the headline rates and oil numbers - the market was already getting more selective before this week's shock made financing costs less predictable too.

themes.title: Oil breaks $100 as the war widens
themes.thesis: The conflict just crossed a line oil traders had been pricing as unlikely - a supply-threatening regional war, not a contained strike campaign.
themes.synthesis: Brent crude settled at $100.69 Thursday, up about 7% and its highest close since May, after Iranian forces struck Kuwait's Al-Salem airbase and Houthi militants hit two Saudi Arabian tankers in the Red Sea - the first confirmed Houthi tanker strikes since the blockade threat surfaced. It was the 13th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran; Trump told Axios he's 'considering a massive attack' and is 'close to making a decision.' The UN Secretary-General called the situation 'getting out of control,' and the US Senate failed to advance a resolution rebuking Trump over the war, though the House passed a measure to limit his war powers.
themes.new_today: Oil crossed $100 for the first time since May; Iran struck a Kuwaiti target for the first time; the Houthis carried out their first confirmed tanker strikes.
themes.why_it_matters: This is no longer a contained strike campaign against one country - it's now touching Kuwait and Saudi shipping directly, which is exactly the supply-shock scenario that breaks inflation forecasts and CRE underwriting models alike.

themes.title: Hike odds double overnight as stocks take the hit
themes.thesis: Markets stopped treating the oil shock as background noise and started pricing it directly into the Fed decision.
themes.synthesis: The S&P 500 fell 1.21% to 7,408.30 and the Nasdaq dropped 2.15% to 25,137.69 Thursday, with Tesla down 14.5% on a profit and free-cash-flow miss despite a 25% delivery jump, and Alphabet down 7% after raising its 2026 capex guidance to $195-205 billion from $180-190 billion. The 10-year Treasury yield jumped to 4.70%, its highest since January 2025. CME FedWatch hike-odds readings moved from roughly 16.6% Wednesday to about 35% by Thursday - more than double in a single session.
themes.new_today: The 1%+ equity selloff, the 10-year's move to a post-January-2025 high, and hike odds more than doubling are all new since Wednesday's close.
themes.why_it_matters: Five days from the meeting, the Fed now has to weigh a genuine oil-driven inflation shock against an economy that was cooling on its own terms - a much harder call than it looked to be facing a week ago.

themes.title: CRE deal volume down 24%, and the Fed just got less predictable
themes.thesis: Deal volume was already thinning before this week's oil shock complicated the rate picture further.
themes.synthesis: CBRE's Q2 2026 capital markets update shows US commercial real estate deal volume down 24% year over year, with construction spending running 2.7% below plan and hiring slowing across the sector. The report frames the second half of 2026 as favoring disciplined underwriting and careful capitalization over volume - guidance issued before this week's oil-driven jump in the 10-year yield, which now makes that caution look prescient rather than conservative.
themes.new_today: The 24% deal-volume decline and the construction-spending shortfall are new data points from CBRE's Q2 update.
themes.why_it_matters: This is the environment Alex underwrites in day to day - a market getting more selective right as financing costs get less predictable, not more.

themes.title: Odds move on LeBron, even if the timeline doesn't
themes.thesis: Betting markets are now doing what the Heat's own leak couldn't - actually pricing the outcome.
themes.synthesis: The Heat confirmed the leaked 'LeBron James Introductory Press Conference' YouTube page, dated July 27, was real prep work built in case of a signing, published by mistake rather than fabricated from nothing. Kalshi bettors treated the leak as signal anyway, pushing Miami's odds of landing James to 51%, up from 39% beforehand. His agent, Rich Paul, says there's still no timetable for a decision, with Cleveland and Philadelphia the other two teams in the mix.
themes.new_today: The Kalshi odds move to 51% and the framing of the page as 'prepared, not fabricated' are both new since Wednesday.
themes.why_it_matters: A roster already hard-capped after the Giannis trade would need real subtraction to make room - odds moving this fast suggest someone close to the situation thinks that math is being worked out.

sources.headline: Heat confirm the LeBron James introductory press conference YouTube video was published accidentally after latest rumors
Miami Heat Get Major Clarity on LeBron James Decision

themes.title: Nine losses later, a chance to actually answer something
themes.thesis: An off day doesn't fix anything on its own - tonight's opener against the Padres is the actual test.
themes.synthesis: Miami (52-51, 8.5 games back in the NL East) opens a three-game series at home against the Padres tonight at loanDepot Park, first pitch 7:10 PM, with German Marquez starting for San Diego. It's the first game since Wednesday's sweep-completing loss extended the skid to nine, and the first real read on whether Thursday's off day changed anything.
themes.new_today: The series opener, tonight's matchup, and the 8.5-game deficit are specific to today.
themes.why_it_matters: The wild-card cushion built in June is gone; this series is close to must-win territory if the season is still meant to mean something in September.

sources.headline: Miami Marlins vs. San Diego Padres Series July 24-26: Odds, Starting Pitchers & Predictions

themes.title: A hurricane, a rebuild, and a hundred years of Art Deco
themes.thesis: Miami Beach's most recognizable identity was never the plan - it was what got built after the plan got destroyed.
themes.synthesis: The Great Miami Hurricane of 1926 leveled much of Miami Beach; the pastel, geometric Art Deco buildings that came to define the neighborhood didn't start appearing until 1933, built by architects borrowing forms from ocean liners and machines. Barbara Capitman and a group of friends founded the Miami Design Preservation League in 1976, as a bicentennial project, to save those same buildings from demolition when they were considered too new and too modest to matter; the district made the National Register in 1979, the first 20th-century historic district in the country and now the largest concentration of Art Deco buildings anywhere.
themes.new_today: Outside the feed: a century since the storm that forced the rebuild, and a half-century since the League that saved what got built.
themes.why_it_matters: A useful corrective on a week that's mostly been about oil and odds - the thing everyone now recognizes as Miami Beach's identity was almost torn down twice, once by a hurricane and once by neglect.

sources.headline: Intro to Art Deco on Miami Beach

themes.title: Miami Takeover opens, and the Adnan mural's last weekend arrives
themes.thesis: Two different Miami culture stories are peaking at once - a four-day party starting tonight, and a nearly year-long exhibition ending Sunday.
themes.synthesis: Miami Takeover's 18th annual weekend runs Friday through Monday across Miami Beach and Downtown, opening tonight with a poolside meet-and-greet at the Z Ocean Hotel and an all-white party at The Urban, followed by Saturday's Art of Go-Go Culture Fest at the Miami Beach Bandshell. Meanwhile, at The Bass, Sarah Crowner's 'Faire Foyer' - built around Etel Adnan's monumental ceramic mural, the only work of its kind by Adnan in the US - closes for good this Sunday, July 26, after nearly a year on view.
themes.new_today: Today is the actual start of Miami Takeover; the Adnan mural now has two days left rather than 'this weekend' in the abstract.
themes.why_it_matters: One of these closes permanently in 48 hours - worth deciding tonight, not Sunday afternoon, which one actually gets your time.

sources.headline: The Miami Takeover is Returning to Miami Beach July 24-27, 2026
Faire Foyer: Sarah Crowner in Dialogue with Etel Adnan

rabbit_hole.title: Uncovering a Global Ancient History Beyond Greece and Rome
rabbit_hole.creator: Carlos Noreña, Aeon
rabbit_hole.why: Noreña, a Berkeley historian, argues the 'ancient world' most of us were taught is a fraction of the real one - the Indian Ocean, not the Mediterranean, was the actual geographic core of premodern Afro-Eurasia. It's the kind of reframe that changes how you read everything else this week without asking anything of you in return.

beautiful_thing.title: The Cross Atop the Tower of Jesus Christ, Sagrada Familia
beautiful_thing.creator: Antoni Gaudi (design); completed by the Sagrada Familia workshop
beautiful_thing.era: Designed in the early 1900s; installed February 20, 2026
beautiful_thing.body: The four-armed cross crowning Barcelona's Sagrada Familia is 17 meters tall and 13.5 meters wide, clad in white enameled ceramic and three layers of curved laminated glass, cut and polished so the surface catches light from different angles. Gaudi specified, in his own words, that it should shine by day and give light by night. The cross is hollow, as he intended, so visitors can climb inside it for a 180-degree view over the city he never lived to see it finished over.

noticing_prompt.text: Notice one thing today that was built to outlast the person who built it - a building, a garden, a room someone else will inherit - and what that changes about how carefully it was made.

evening_quote.text: Nowhere can a man find a quieter or more untroubled retreat than in his own soul.

evening_unwind.evening_prompt: What looked like a crisis by nine this morning, and how much of it will still be worth mentioning by name a week from now?

quote.text: We suffer more often in imagination than in reality.

threads.title: US-Iran conflict and the Strait of Hormuz
threads.current_state: Brent settled at $100.69 Thursday, up about 7% and the highest since May, after Iran struck Kuwait's Al-Salem airbase and the Houthis hit two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea - the 13th consecutive night of US strikes. Trump says he is considering a 'massive attack' on Iran; the UN Secretary-General warns the situation is 'getting out of control.'

timeline.summary: First tracked: nine nights of US strikes, oil up about 20% this month, 10-day ceasefire being mediated.
10-day ceasefire formally proposed; US strikes continue, Iran calls it full-scale war, Brent tops $91, war cost estimated at $37.5B.
By Wednesday, strikes reach an eleventh consecutive night; Brent pushes past $92, reviving inflation concern.

Ceasefire proposal remains unadopted; Brent closes at $94.13, a five-week high; Strait transits fall to 25 ships from a peak of 49; Saudi coalition vows a forceful response to the Houthi blockade threat.

13th consecutive night of strikes; Iran hits Kuwait's Al-Salem airbase, Houthis strike two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea; Brent crosses $100 for the first time since May; Trump considers a 'massive attack'; UN warns the situation is getting out of control.

threads.title: Federal Reserve rate path

The 10Y jumped to 4.70%, its highest since January 2025. CME FedWatch hike odds surged to roughly 35% from 16.6% a day earlier.

Fed week arrives; positioning visible in Friday's chip-led selloff.

Hike odds tick back up to 16.6% as oil keeps climbing; stocks rally anyway on chip earnings; June CPI confirmed at 3.5%.

Wednesday's close confirmed as a reversal of Tuesday's rally (S&P -0.14%, Nasdaq -0.56%); 10Y up to 4.66%; FOMC odds still dispersed across sources.
Sharp selloff on oil breaking $100 plus Tesla/Alphabet earnings: S&P -1.21%, Nasdaq -2.15%; 10Y to 4.70%, highest since Jan 2025; hike odds more than double to roughly 35%.
